turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: In Case 23-334, Department of State versus Muñoz, Justice Barrett has the opinion of the Court.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: In 2010, Sandra Munoz, an American citizen, married Luis Asencio-Cordero, a citizen of El Salvador. The couple sought to obtain an immigrant visa for Asencio-Cordero so that they could live together in the United States. To obtain a visa, Asencio-Cordero submitted an application to the United States Consulate in San Salvador, and he met with a consular officer for several interviews. The officer ultimately denied Asencio-Cordero’s application, citing a statute that bars non-citizens from being admitted to the United States if the officer knows or has reasonable ground to believe that they seek to enter the United States to engage in unlawful activity. Asencio-Cordero suspected that he was denied a visa based on a finding that he was a member of MS-13, a transnational criminal gang. He denied membership in any gang, and he and Munoz appealed the officer’s finding to the State Department, which agreed with the consulate’s determination. Sandra Munoz then sued the State Department, arguing that the constitution required it to divulge more information about why it found her husband inadmissible under the unlawful activity bar. The District Court agreed that the State Department needed to reveal more, but after reviewing confidential law enforcement information, identifying Asencio-Cordero as a member of MS-13, the District Court granted summary judgment to the State Department. Munoz appealed to the Ninth Circuit, which vacated the judgment. It held that the State Department had offered too little, too late, and that the District Court should review the consular officer’s finding that Asencio-Cordero was a member of MS-13, and potentially ordered the State Department to re-adjudicate his visa application. The Ninth Circuit was wrong. Like many questions of constitutional law, this one boils down to who decides. And under the century-old doctrine of consular non-reviewability, the answer is the Executive Branch. We have long held the consular officer’s decisions to exclude non-citizens from the United States are not subject to judicial review. Federal courts are not equipped to set immigration policy and manage border security. Those jobs are entrusted to Congress and the President. Munoz argues that an exception applies to her situation. She says that the State Department has burdened her fundamental right to marriage by refusing her husband permission to live with her in the United States. Munoz certainly has a fundamental right to marriage. She is after all married, but she does not have a fundamental right to immigrate her spouse to the United States. On the contrary, whether one thinks this is advisable or inadvisable, Congress has regulated and restricted spousal immigration since it first began regulating immigration. And while Congress has sometimes extended special treatment to marriage, neither Congress nor the Constitution has ever made spousal immigration a matter of right. The Court has not interfered with such policy choices of Congress and the executive, even when those policies have interfered with the spousal relationship. A case called Knauff versus Shaughnessy involved facts similar to this one. In that case, the spouse of an American citizen was denied admission to the United States for confidential security reasons. The citizen protested the decision, seeking both the reasons for it and an opportunity to challenge it. The Court declined to look behind the decision, holding instead that both the process and its results lay in the conclusive authority of the political branches. As we have explained in other cases, the families of those who seek to immigrate to the United States have an interest in their admission, but it’s a fallacy to leap from that premise to the conclusion that United States citizens have a fundamental right that can limit how Congress exercises the nation’s sovereign power to admit or exclude foreigners. And so we end where we began. Federal courts are not equipped to set immigration policy and manage border security. Those jobs are entrusted to Congress and the President. And under the doctrine of consular non-reviewability, the federal courts cannot review visa decisions made by consular officers. We reverse the judgment below and remand the case for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. Justice Gorsuch has filed an opinion concurring in the judgment. Justice Sotomayor has filed a dissenting opinion, which Justice Kagan and Justice Jackson have joined.
